title: Counted sub-structures (sub : List<T>)
analysis:
This task implements counted sub-structure collections (e.g., `sub bolts : List<Bolt>`) with count constraints that trigger schema re-elaboration. The implementation spans 5 modules:

1. **Grammar (tree-sitter-reify)**: The current `sub_declaration` rule only supports `sub name = StructName<TypeArgs>(args)`. The spec requires `sub name : List<StructName>` form. We need to extend the grammar rule to support a collection form using `choice()` — the existing `=` instantiation form plus a new `:` collection-type form.

2. **Syntax (reify-syntax)**: `SubDecl` needs an `is_collection: bool` field. The parser's `lower_sub()` must detect the collection form (when the grammar produces a node with the colon/List form) and set `is_collection=true`, extracting the element type name as structure_name.

3. **Compiler (reify-compiler)**: `SubComponentDecl` needs `is_collection: bool` and `count_cell: Option<ValueCellId>`. When `is_collection` is true, the compiler must create a synthetic count ValueCell (e.g., `S.__count_bolts`, type Int, Let kind) and recognize `constraint bolts.count == expr` as structure-controlling (adding the count cell to `structure_controlling`). The count cell's expression comes from the constraint RHS.

4. **Eval graph (reify-eval/graph.rs)**: `from_templates()` must handle collection sub-components. If `is_collection` and count is determined (Int(N)), create N scoped instances: `Parent.name[0]`, `Parent.name[1]`, etc. Each instance gets full value cells from the child template. If count is Undef, no instances are created.

5. **Eval engine (reify-eval/lib.rs)**: Count re-elaboration — when the count ValueCell's value changes, trigger graph topology change: add/remove instances to match new count. This follows the existing guard re-elaboration pattern.

**Review Fix Analysis (steps 21-26):**

- **Issue 1 (unnecessary_full_collection_rebuild)**: The count-change re-elaboration at lib.rs:1625-1630 rebuilds the entire ValueMap O(V_total) on every count change. PersistentMap already has remove(), but ValueMap doesn't expose it. Fix: add ValueMap::remove() and replace the full rebuild with surgical removal of stale instance entries O(count_diff × child_cells).

- **Issues 2+4 (incorrect_type_annotation + missing assertion)**: The compiler hardcodes `result_type: Type::Real` at lines 896 and 907 for all indexed collection member accesses regardless of the member's actual type. Fix: extend CompilationScope to store a mapping from (collection_name, member_name) → Type by looking up already-compiled child templates. Pass `&[TopologyTemplate]` to compile_entity. Use this to resolve the correct member type. The existing test also lacks a result_type assertion.

- **Issue 3 (declaration_order_dependency)**: When `constraint bolts.count == n` appears before `sub bolts : List<Bolt>` in source order, the single-pass member processing can't find the SubComponentDecl to set count_cell (it hasn't been pushed yet). Fix: add a reconciliation sweep after the second pass that backfills count_cell for any unlinked __count_ cells.

Key existing patterns to reuse:
- Guard re-elaboration pattern (structure_controlling -> dirty check -> re-evaluate)
- Sub-component scoping pattern (Parent.sub_name -> scoped ValueCellIds)
- TopologyTemplateBuilder for test construction
- `.count`, `.map()`, `.all()` method calls already implemented in reify-expr
- PersistentMap::remove() already exists, just needs ValueMap wrapper

The `forall b in bolts: predicate` syntax doesn't exist in the grammar; tests will use `.all(|b| predicate)` instead, which is already supported in the expression evaluator.

prerequisites.description: Extend tree-sitter grammar: add collection form to sub_declaration. Change the rule from a single seq() to choice() with two alternatives: (1) existing `sub name = StructName<TypeArgs>(args)` form, and (2) new `sub name : List < StructName >` form using `:` separator. The collection form rule: seq('sub', field('name', $.identifier), ':', 'List', '<', field('structure_name', $.identifier), '>', optional(field('guard', $.where_clause))). Then run `cd tree-sitter-reify && tree-sitter generate` to regenerate parser.c, grammar.json, and node-types.json. Verify grammar compiles and existing sub tests still parse.

Add `is_collection: bool` field to SubDecl in reify-syntax/src/lib.rs. Initialize to `false` in the existing SubDecl construction site in lower_sub() (ts_parser.rs line 945). This is a prerequisite for TDD steps since the struct change must exist before tests can reference it.

Add `is_collection: bool` and `count_cell: Option<ValueCellId>` fields to SubComponentDecl in reify-compiler/src/lib.rs. Initialize to `false`/`None` in all existing SubComponentDecl construction sites: (1) compile_entity Sub handler around line 1883, (2) connector sub in compile_connection around line 2769. Update TopologyTemplateBuilder.sub_component() in reify-test-support/builders.rs to include `is_collection: false, count_cell: None`. Ensure all existing code compiles cleanly with the new fields.

steps.description: Write failing test in crates/reify-compiler/tests/collection_sub_tests.rs: parse `structure S { sub bolts : List<Bolt> }` using reify_syntax::parse(). Assert the parsed SubDecl has is_collection==true, structure_name=='Bolt', name=='bolts'. Also test that `structure S { sub rib = Rib() }` produces is_collection==false.

Implement collection sub parsing in ts_parser.rs lower_sub(). Detect if the sub_declaration node uses the collection form (has 'List' keyword and ':' separator). This can be detected by checking for a child matching 'List' text or by checking whether the '=' or ':' token is present. When collection form: set is_collection=true and extract structure_name from the element type field. When standard `=` form: set is_collection=false as before.

Write failing test: compile `structure Bolt { param diameter : Scalar = 10mm } structure S { sub bolts : List<Bolt> }` and assert the compiled SubComponentDecl has is_collection==true and structure_name=='Bolt'. Also verify existing `sub rib = Rib()` compiles with is_collection==false.

Propagate is_collection from SubDecl to SubComponentDecl in compiler. In compile_entity() where SubComponentDecl is constructed (around line 1883), copy is_collection from the parsed SubDecl. Collection subs have no args (empty vec) since the `List<T>` form doesn't take constructor args.

Write failing test: compile `structure S { param n : Int = 4  sub bolts : List<Bolt>  constraint bolts.count == n }`. Assert: (1) a synthetic count ValueCell '__count_bolts' exists in template.value_cells with type Int and Let kind, (2) its ID is in template.structure_controlling, (3) its default_expr is a ValueRef to 'n'.

Implement count constraint recognition in the compiler. When compiling constraints in compile_entity(), detect pattern `<collection_name>.count == <expr>` where the LHS is a MemberAccess with member='count' and object matching a collection sub-component name. Create a synthetic Let ValueCell `{entity}.__count_{name}` (Int type, expr = constraint RHS), add it to structure_controlling. Store the count cell ID in SubComponentDecl.count_cell. The constraint itself is still compiled normally.

Write failing test in crates/reify-eval/tests/collection_sub_eval.rs: Build module with Parent (param n : Int = 4, count_cell __count_bolts with expr=ValueRef(n), collection sub bolts: List<Bolt>) and Child (Bolt with param diameter). Use TopologyTemplateBuilder with new collection_sub_component() method. After EvaluationGraph::from_templates(), assert 4 scoped ValueCellNodes exist: Parent.bolts[0].diameter through Parent.bolts[3].diameter.

Add collection_sub_component() method to TopologyTemplateBuilder that creates SubComponentDecl with is_collection=true and count_cell. Add a structure_controlling_cell() method to add arbitrary IDs to structure_controlling. Implement collection elaboration in EvaluationGraph::from_templates(): when sub.is_collection and sub.count_cell is Some, check if the count cell's value can be statically determined (look up the count cell in the graph); for builder-based tests, create N instances based on the count cell's default_expr literal value. Each instance has scoped IDs `Parent.name[i]` with child template value cells.

Write failing test: Engine.eval() with collection count=4 produces 4 Bolt instances with their own values. Build module with Parent (n=4, collection sub bolts) and Bolt (param diameter=10mm). After eval(), assert Parent.bolts[0].diameter through Parent.bolts[3].diameter have correct values in snapshot. Also assert the count cell __count_bolts has value Int(4).

Implement collection sub-component evaluation in Engine.eval(). In the sub-component elaboration phase (around line 830), handle is_collection subs: (1) evaluate the count cell to determine N, (2) if count is Int(N), elaborate N instances of the child template with scoped entity prefix `Parent.name[i]`, (3) evaluate each instance's params with arg defaults from the child template, (4) evaluate each instance's let bindings. Skip elaboration if count is Undef.

Write failing test: count Undef means no instances. Build module where count cell has no default (or depends on an Undef param). After eval(), assert no scoped value cells exist for bolts[i] instances.

Handle Undef count in collection elaboration. In both from_templates() and Engine.eval(), when count cell evaluates to Undef or non-Int, skip instance creation. The count cell itself remains in the snapshot with its Undef value.

Write failing test: count change 4->6 triggers re-elaboration. Build module with Parent (param n=4, collection sub bolts, count cell __count_bolts = n). Initial eval with n=4 produces 4 instances. edit_param(n, Int(6)). Assert: (1) topology_fingerprint changes, (2) 6 instances now exist (bolts[0] through bolts[5]), (3) new instances bolts[4] and bolts[5] have correct value cells.

Implement count-based re-elaboration in Engine's incremental eval. In the guard re-elaboration phase (around line 1276), extend to also handle collection count cells. When a structure_controlling cell is a count cell and its value changed: (1) determine old vs new count, (2) remove old scoped instances from graph and values, (3) create new instances based on new count, (4) evaluate new instances, (5) recompute topology fingerprint. Also handle in the parallel incremental_eval path (around line 1508).

Write failing test: bolts[0].diameter access via compiled expression. Parse and compile `structure Bolt { param diameter : Scalar = 10mm } structure S { sub bolts : List<Bolt>  constraint bolts.count == 4  let d = bolts[0].diameter }`. Assert: the let binding 'd' compiles to a ValueRef or IndexAccess expression that can resolve to Parent.bolts[0].diameter.

Implement indexed collection member access in the compiler. When compiling expression `name[i].member` where `name` is a collection sub: if `i` is a literal integer, resolve to ValueRef with scoped ID `Parent.name[i].member`. If `i` is a non-literal expression, compile as IndexAccess+MemberAccess chain (dynamic indexing). Register collection sub names in CompilationScope so the compiler can distinguish collection access from regular index access.

Write failing test: `let n = bolts.count` compiles and evaluates to the count value. Parse and compile a structure with collection sub and `let n = bolts.count`. Eval and assert n == Int(4). This should compile bolts.count as a ValueRef to the __count_bolts cell.

Implement bolts.count expression compilation. When the compiler sees MemberAccess with object being a collection sub name and member being 'count', compile it as a ValueRef to the count cell `__count_{name}`. This is simpler than a MethodCall since the count cell already exists as a Let binding.

Write integration test: collection operations on sub-structure instances. Build module using builders: Parent with collection sub bolts (count=3), Bolt with param grade (default=8.8). Create a let binding that gathers bolt grades into a list and calls .all(). Verify eval produces correct results. Note: this tests that collection method dispatch works on sub-structure instance lists, using the existing .all() method on List values.

Implement collection value aggregation. When a collection sub name is referenced in an expression context (not as .count or [i].member), create a synthetic List value by gathering instance values. Add logic in Engine.eval() (or as a Let binding in from_templates()) that for each collection sub with determined count, creates a List<StructureRef> value from instance references. This enables .map(), .all(), .any() etc. to work on the collection.

[REVIEW FIX: unnecessary_full_collection_rebuild] Write test for ValueMap::remove() in crates/reify-types/src/value.rs (unit test in existing #[cfg(test)] mod tests block). Insert 3 entries, remove the middle one, assert it's gone and the other two remain. Also write an eval test in crates/reify-eval/tests/collection_sub_eval.rs: count change 4→2, then verify that bolts[0] and bolts[1] values remain in the snapshot but bolts[2] and bolts[3] are gone (not just overwritten). This catches the O(V_total) rebuild behavior — the test itself will pass with the current impl since correctness is maintained, but the impl step will replace the rebuild with surgical removal.

[REVIEW FIX: unnecessary_full_collection_rebuild] Add `pub fn remove(&mut self, id: &ValueCellId)` method to ValueMap in crates/reify-types/src/value.rs, delegating to `self.inner.remove(id)`. Then in crates/reify-eval/src/lib.rs, replace the 7-line full rebuild at lines 1625-1630 (`let mut new_values = ValueMap::new(); for (id, (val, _)) in new_snapshot.values.iter() { new_values.insert(...) } values = new_values;`) with surgical removal: in the old-instance removal loop (lines 1560-1567), add `values.remove(&scoped_id)` alongside the existing `new_snapshot.graph.value_cells.remove()` and `new_snapshot.values.remove()` calls. This reduces per-count-change work from O(V_total) to O(count_diff × child_cells).

[REVIEW FIX: incorrect_type_annotation + missing_meaningful_assertion] In crates/reify-compiler/tests/collection_sub_tests.rs: (1) Update the existing `compile_indexed_collection_member_access` test to add `assert_eq!(expr.result_type, reify_types::Type::Real)` after the ValueRef match — this documents the current (incorrect) behavior and will be changed to the correct type assertion in the impl step. (2) Add a new test `compile_indexed_collection_member_access_preserves_type` that compiles `structure Bolt { param count_per_row : Int = 4 } structure S { sub bolts : List<Bolt> constraint bolts.count == 4 let c = bolts[0].count_per_row }` and asserts `expr.result_type == Type::Int`. This test will FAIL because the compiler currently hardcodes Type::Real for all indexed member accesses.

[REVIEW FIX: incorrect_type_annotation] Fix the hardcoded `Type::Real` at lines 896 and 907 of crates/reify-compiler/src/lib.rs. Approach: (1) Add `collection_sub_member_types: HashMap<String, HashMap<String, Type>>` field to CompilationScope (maps collection_name → { member_name → Type }). (2) Add `compiled_templates: &[TopologyTemplate]` parameter to compile_entity(). Update both call sites in compile() (lines 1394, 1423) to pass `&templates`. (3) In the first-pass Sub registration (around line 1701), when is_collection is true, look up the child template by structure_name from compiled_templates, iterate its value_cells, and populate collection_sub_member_types. (4) In compile_expr_guarded MemberAccess handling (line 896), replace `Type::Real` with a lookup: `scope.collection_sub_member_types.get(name).and_then(|m| m.get(member)).cloned().unwrap_or(Type::Real)`. Apply the same fix at line 907 for the dynamic-index path. (5) Update the step-23 test assertions: change the existing test's assertion to the correct type (Scalar for diameter), and confirm the Int-typed test passes.

[REVIEW FIX: declaration_order_dependency] In crates/reify-compiler/tests/collection_sub_tests.rs, add test `compile_count_constraint_before_sub_declaration` that compiles: `structure Bolt { param diameter : Scalar = 10mm } structure S { param n : Int = 4  constraint bolts.count == n  sub bolts : List<Bolt> }` (note: constraint appears BEFORE the sub declaration). Assert: (1) the SubComponentDecl for 'bolts' has count_cell == Some with member '__count_bolts', (2) the __count_bolts cell exists in structure_controlling. This test will FAIL because the current single-pass code at line 1874 does `sub_components.iter_mut().find(|s| s.name == coll_name)` which returns None when the sub hasn't been pushed yet.

[REVIEW FIX: declaration_order_dependency] In crates/reify-compiler/src/lib.rs, add a reconciliation sweep after the second member-processing pass (after the `for member in structure.members` loop ends, before the template is constructed). For each ValueCellDecl in value_cells whose member name matches `__count_{name}` pattern, find the corresponding SubComponentDecl in sub_components where sub.name == name AND sub.count_cell.is_none(). If found, backfill sub.count_cell = Some(count_cell_id). This handles the case where the count constraint was processed before the sub declaration was pushed. The reconciliation is O(count_cells × sub_components) which is negligible.

design_decisions.decision: Extend tree-sitter grammar with dedicated collection sub form using choice() rather than reusing existing = syntax with List as structure_name
design_decisions.rationale: The spec defines `sub name : List<StructName>` with `:` syntax, distinct from `sub name = StructName(args)` with `=`. Adding a proper grammar alternative makes the two forms syntactically distinct and avoids conflating List (a collection wrapper) with concrete structure names. The tree-sitter grammar change is straightforward: a choice() between the two seq() alternatives in sub_declaration.

design_decisions.decision: Synthetic count ValueCell (`__count_{name}`) as a Let binding in structure_controlling
design_decisions.rationale: This follows the existing guard cell pattern where structure_controlling contains ValueCellIds whose changes trigger re-elaboration. The count cell is a Let binding (computed, not externally settable) whose expression comes from the `constraint name.count == expr` pattern. When the expression's dependencies change, the count re-evaluates, triggering re-elaboration if the count value differs.

design_decisions.decision: Instance scoping uses bracket notation: Parent.name[i] as the entity prefix
design_decisions.rationale: This follows the spec's positional indexing (`vents[0]`, `vents[1]`) and extends the existing scoping pattern (Parent.sub_name becomes Parent.name[i]). Each instance's value cells are: ValueCellId { entity: 'Parent.bolts[0]', member: 'diameter' }. This is natural, unambiguous, and compatible with the existing sub-component scoping.

design_decisions.decision: Count re-elaboration follows the existing guard re-elaboration pattern
design_decisions.rationale: The guard re-elaboration code already handles structure_controlling cells changing and triggering topology updates. Collection count changes are analogous: a structure_controlling cell (the count) changes value, requiring the graph topology to change (add/remove instances). Reusing this pattern keeps the implementation consistent and leverages battle-tested code.

design_decisions.decision: forall syntax not implemented; use .all(|x| predicate) instead
design_decisions.rationale: The `forall b in bolts: predicate` syntax has no grammar production and would require significant grammar/parser/compiler work. The existing .all() method on List values (already implemented in reify-expr eval_method_call) provides equivalent functionality. The spec's forall can be added as a separate syntax sugar task.

design_decisions.decision: Add count_cell: Option<ValueCellId> to SubComponentDecl rather than a separate lookup table
design_decisions.rationale: Keeping the count association co-located with the sub-component declaration avoids a separate lookup structure. For non-collection subs, count_cell is None. For collection subs, it points to the synthetic __count_ ValueCell. This makes graph construction straightforward: the count cell is directly available from the SubComponentDecl.

design_decisions.decision: Collection sub args are empty (no constructor arguments in List<T> form)
design_decisions.rationale: The `sub name : List<T>` form doesn't take constructor arguments. Individual instances get their defaults from the child template. Per-instance customization would be done through constraints on indexed members (e.g., `constraint bolts[0].grade == 10.9`), not through constructor args at the collection level.

design_decisions.decision: Surgical ValueMap removal instead of full rebuild for count-change re-elaboration
design_decisions.rationale: The full O(V_total) ValueMap rebuild on every count change is unnecessary. PersistentMap already has a remove() method. Adding ValueMap::remove() and using it to surgically delete stale instance entries reduces per-count-change cost to O(count_diff × child_cells). This is important because count changes are expected to be frequent during interactive editing.

design_decisions.decision: Look up collection member types from already-compiled templates rather than defaulting to Type::Real
design_decisions.rationale: Hardcoding Type::Real for all indexed collection member accesses (bolts[0].diameter) is incorrect when the member has type Int, Bool, Length, etc. The compiler processes templates sequentially, so child templates (e.g., Bolt) are compiled before parents (e.g., S) when declared first. Passing &[TopologyTemplate] to compile_entity allows looking up child member types. For forward-reference cases (child declared after parent), Type::Real remains as a fallback, matching existing forward-reference behavior.

design_decisions.decision: Post-pass reconciliation sweep for count_cell backfill rather than a third member pass
design_decisions.rationale: When a count constraint appears before the sub declaration in source order, the single-pass processing can't find the SubComponentDecl to set count_cell. A lightweight reconciliation sweep after the member loop (matching __count_{name} cells to sub_components) is simpler and more targeted than restructuring the compiler into multiple passes. The sweep is O(count_cells × sub_components) which is negligible.

reuse.what: Guard re-elaboration pattern (structure_controlling, dirty check, branch flip)
reuse.how: Extend the existing guard re-elaboration phase to also handle collection count changes. When a structure_controlling cell is a count cell (not a guard cell), apply count-based re-elaboration (add/remove instances) instead of guard branch flipping.
Extend for indexed instances: `Parent.name[i]` instead of `Parent.name`. Each instance follows the same scoping logic: child template's value cells get scoped IDs with the indexed entity prefix.
Add collection_sub_component() method that creates SubComponentDecl with is_collection=true and a count_cell parameter. Add structure_controlling_cell() to register count cells.
Use to build test modules with both parent (collection sub) and child (element structure) templates.

reuse.what: Collection method calls (.count, .map, .all, .sum) in expression evaluator
reuse.how: The expression evaluator already handles method calls on List values. Once collection instances are gathered into a List value, all existing collection operations (.count, .map, .all, .any, .sum, .fold) work naturally.
Reuse the same parse+compile+assert-no-errors helper pattern for collection sub compilation tests.
Run `cd tree-sitter-reify && tree-sitter generate` after editing grammar.js to regenerate parser.c, grammar.json, and node-types.json.
PersistentMap already has a remove() method that delegates to ImHashMap::remove(). ValueMap wraps PersistentMap but doesn't expose remove(). Adding ValueMap::remove() is a one-line delegation.
